title: Why we keep visiting the same coffee shop
summary: Reflect on what draws people back to the same small place repeatedly, and what that loyalty says about comfort, routine, and belonging.
topic: Why we keep visiting the same coffee shop
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) reflecting on why people return to the same coffee shop or café again and again. Use a real or imagined example, include the present perfect to show ongoing habit, at least one conditional sentence, and a concession with 'although' or 'even though'.
hintStarters: There is a coffee shop on a narrow street in my neighbourhood that I have been going to for…
Most people could probably find cheaper coffee nearby, but what keeps them coming back is…
If the place ever closed, I think I would feel…

hintVocab.meaningVi: khách quen, người thường xuyên lui tới
cảm giác thuộc về nơi đó
người thích thói quen, sống theo nếp
làm điểm tựa cho ngày của bạn

hintVocab.phrase: to step away from the noise

hintVocab.meaningVi: trí nhớ cơ bắp, thói quen tự nhiên
cảm thấy được nhận ra, được chú ý
một nghi thức, thói quen có ý nghĩa
tìm thấy sự an ủi trong điều gì đó

sampleText:
There is a coffee shop two streets from where I grew up in Porto, run by a retired teacher who has been making the same espresso blend since 1998. I started going there at seventeen, mostly because it was close and cheap. Now, twenty years later, I still go every Saturday morning, and I have never once ordered anything different. When people ask me why, I usually say the coffee is good — which is true — but I know that is not really the reason.

Although there are newer places nearby with better light and faster Wi-Fi, none of them gives me the same feeling of stepping out of the week and into something quieter. The owner knows my name and knows I do not want to talk before the second cup. That small act of being known is worth more than any trendy menu. I think what keeps people returning to one particular café is not the product — it is the ritual around the product. The walk there, the usual seat by the window, the same order placed without even thinking: these are the pieces of a routine that anchors the rest of the day. If the shop ever disappeared, I would lose not just a place for coffee but a reliable marker in the week, something I have taken comfort in without fully noticing.

What I have come to understand is that we do not choose a regular café the way we choose a restaurant for a special occasion. We stumble into it, go back a second time because it was fine, and then one day realise it has become part of our identity. This is especially true in cities, where the surrounding environment changes quickly and small, consistent places become rare. People who have moved far from home — from Seoul or Lagos or Guadalajara — often talk about finding one café that feels steady, and holding on to it. The loyalty is not sentimental in an embarrassing way. It is just a quiet acknowledgment that, in a life full of change, some small things are worth keeping exactly as they are.

bước ra khỏi tuần làm việc
vào một thứ gì đó yên tĩnh hơn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: anchors the rest of the day

tìm thấy sự an ủi trong
mà không hoàn toàn nhận ra
một quán cà phê quen thuộc
chúng ta tình cờ bước vào đó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: it has become part of our identity
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nó đã trở thành một phần bản sắc của chúng ta
những nơi nhỏ bé nhưng nhất quán

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for long-running habit
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: '…who has been making the same espresso blend since 1998' và 'I have never once ordered anything different' dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về một thói quen kéo dài đến tận hiện tại — dùng 'since' cho điểm bắt đầu, 'never' để nhấn mạnh tính nhất quán.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: '…who has been making the same espresso blend since 1998' and 'I have never once ordered anything different' both use the present perfect to describe a habit that extends up to now — 'since' marks the starting point, and 'never' emphasises consistency.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for imagined loss
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If the shop ever disappeared, I would lose not just a place for coffee but a reliable marker in the week' dùng quá khứ đơn trong mệnh đề 'if' và 'would + động từ nguyên thể' trong mệnh đề chính để nói về một tình huống giả định — điều chưa xảy ra nhưng có thể xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If the shop ever disappeared, I would lose not just a place for coffee but a reliable marker in the week' uses past simple in the if-clause and 'would + base verb' in the main clause to describe a hypothetical situation — something that has not happened but could.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một quán cà phê cách hai con phố từ nơi tôi lớn lên ở Porto, do một giáo viên đã nghỉ hưu điều hành — người đã pha cùng một loại hỗn hợp espresso đó từ năm 1998. Tôi bắt đầu đến đó năm mười bảy tuổi, chủ yếu vì nó gần và rẻ. Giờ đây, hai mươi năm sau, tôi vẫn đến mỗi sáng thứ Bảy, và chưa bao giờ gọi món gì khác. Khi mọi người hỏi tôi tại sao, tôi thường nói cà phê ngon — điều đó đúng — nhưng tôi biết đó không thực sự là lý do.

Mặc dù có những nơi mới hơn gần đó với ánh sáng tốt hơn và Wi-Fi nhanh hơn, không nơi nào mang lại cho tôi cảm giác bước ra khỏi tuần làm việc và vào một thứ gì đó yên tĩnh hơn. Chủ quán biết tên tôi và biết tôi không muốn nói chuyện trước tách cà phê thứ hai. Hành động nhỏ đó — được nhận ra — có giá trị hơn bất kỳ thực đơn nào theo xu hướng. Tôi nghĩ điều giữ chân mọi người quay lại một quán cà phê cụ thể không phải là sản phẩm — mà là nghi thức xung quanh sản phẩm. Đường đi đến đó, chiếc ghế quen bên cửa sổ, món gọi giống nhau mà không cần suy nghĩ: đây là những mảnh ghép của một thói quen làm điểm tựa cho phần còn lại của ngày. Nếu quán đó biến mất, tôi sẽ mất không chỉ một nơi để uống cà phê mà còn mất một cột mốc đáng tin cậy trong tuần, điều tôi đã tìm thấy sự an ủi mà không hoàn toàn nhận ra.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu ra là chúng ta không chọn một quán quen theo cách chọn nhà hàng cho dịp đặc biệt. Chúng ta tình cờ bước vào, quay lại lần thứ hai vì thấy ổn, rồi một ngày nhận ra nó đã trở thành một phần bản sắc của mình. Điều này đặc biệt đúng ở các thành phố, nơi môi trường xung quanh thay đổi nhanh chóng và những nơi nhỏ bé nhất quán trở nên hiếm có. Những người đã rời xa quê hương — từ Seoul, Lagos hay Guadalajara — thường nói về việc tìm thấy một quán cà phê mang lại cảm giác ổn định, và bám víu vào nó. Sự trung thành đó không phải là hoài cảm theo kiểu đáng ngại. Đó chỉ là sự thừa nhận lặng lẽ rằng, trong một cuộc sống đầy thay đổi, một số điều nhỏ bé đáng được giữ nguyên như vậy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a long-running habit?
options: She has been making the same blend since 1998.
She is making the same blend since 1998.
She made the same blend since 1998.
She has make the same blend since 1998.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect continuous ('has been making') with 'since' describes an action that started in the past and continues to the present.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'has/have been + -ing' with 'since' for an ongoing action.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to introduce a concession?
options: Although there are newer places nearby, none of them gives me the same feeling.
Although there are newer places nearby but none of them gives me the same feeling.
Although, there are newer places nearby, none of them gives me the same feeling.
There are newer places although none of them gives me the same feeling nearby.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no extra 'but', no comma directly after 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' introduces a subordinate clause; the main clause follows without 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If the shop ever disappeared, I would lose a reliable marker in the week.
If the shop ever disappeared, I will lose a reliable marker in the week.
If the shop ever disappears, I would lose a reliable marker in the week.
If the shop ever disappeared, I would losing a reliable marker in the week.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + base verb' in the result clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past simple after 'if', then 'would' + bare infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly uses a relative clause?
options: a coffee shop run by a retired teacher who has been making the same blend since 1998
a coffee shop run by a retired teacher which has been making the same blend since 1998
a coffee shop run by a retired teacher who he has been making the same blend since 1998
a coffee shop run by a retired teacher that making the same blend since 1998
mcQuestions.explanation: Use 'who' for a person in a relative clause, and do not repeat the subject with a pronoun like 'he'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Who' refers to people; don't add a second subject pronoun inside the clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'not just… but' to add emphasis?
options: I would lose not just a place for coffee but a reliable marker in the week.
I would lose not just a place for coffee but also however a reliable marker.
I would lose not just a place for coffee, but not a reliable marker in the week.
I would lose not just only a place for coffee but a reliable marker in the week.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not just A but B' is the correct structure — no need for 'also however' or 'only'.
mcQuestions.hint: The pattern is 'not just X but Y' — keep it clean.

options: What keeps people returning to one particular café is the ritual.
What keeps people to return to one particular café is the ritual.
What keeps people return to one particular café is the ritual.
What keeps people that return to one particular café is the ritual.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Keep + gerund' means to continue doing something; 'keep returning' not 'keep to return'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'keep', use the -ing form of the verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'come to + infinitive' correctly for a gradual realisation?
options: What I have come to understand is that we do not choose a regular café the way we choose a restaurant.
What I have come to understanding is that we do not choose a regular café.
What I have come understand is that we do not choose a regular café.
What I have came to understand is that we do not choose a regular café.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have come to + base verb' expresses a gradual realisation; 'come' is the past participle of 'come'.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect of 'come to understand': 'have come to understand' — base verb after 'to'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is the correct collocation for a comforting habit?
options: take comfort in something
take comfort at something
take comfort of something
take comfort by something
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Take comfort in something' is the fixed preposition collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: The preposition that follows 'take comfort' is 'in'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'especially' correctly to add a specific context?
options: This is especially true in cities, where the environment changes quickly.
This is especially true in cities, which the environment changes quickly.
This is especially true in cities, where the environment it changes quickly.
This is specially true in cities, where the environment changes quickly.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Especially' (not 'specially') is the adverb for emphasis; 'where' introduces the relative clause correctly.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Especially' modifies adjectives and adverbs for emphasis; 'where' refers to places in relative clauses.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly expresses a contrast between expectation and reality?
options: Even though the coffee is nothing special, I have never chosen anywhere else.
Even though the coffee is nothing special but I have never chosen anywhere else.
Even the coffee is nothing special, I have never chosen anywhere else.
Even though the coffee is nothing special, I have never choose anywhere else.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even though + clause, main clause' — no extra 'but'; the main clause uses present perfect 'have never chosen'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even though' works like 'although' — no 'but' needed, and check the verb form in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence avoids an unnecessary adverb and stays natural at B1?
options: The loyalty is not sentimental in an embarrassing way.
The loyalty is not very overly sentimentally embarrassing.
The loyalty is not sentimental in a very, very embarrassing way.
The loyalty is absolutely not sentimentally embarrassed.
mcQuestions.explanation: B1 writing should be clear and direct; stacking adverbs like 'very overly' or misusing '-ed' adjectives weakens the sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: Use one precise modifier rather than stacking adverbs.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is loyalty to a familiar place a sign of contentment, or can it become a way of avoiding new experiences? Where is the line between comforting routine and limiting habit?
